event: Following the retreat of glacial ice, the first hunter-gatherers of the Kunda culture settle the territory of present-day Latvia.
Finno-Ugric tribes arrive in the region, becoming the ancestors of the later Livonian people.
Indo-European Baltic tribes settle the area, becoming the ancestors of the Latvians.
The Roman historian Tacitus describes the Aesti, Baltic amber-gathering peoples on the eastern shore of the Baltic Sea.
Distinct Baltic tribal groups including the Latgalians, Selonians, Semigallians, and Couronians emerge across the region.
German merchants from Bremen and Lübeck begin trading along the Daugava River, opening the region to Western contact.
The missionary Meinhard establishes a church at Ikšķile, beginning German Christianization of the Livs.
Bishop Albert founds the city of Riga, which becomes the center of German expansion in the eastern Baltic.
The crusading military order of the Livonian Brothers of the Sword is established to subjugate the Baltic tribes.
After heavy losses, the Brothers of the Sword merge with the Teutonic Order, forming the Livonian Order.
Riga joins the Hanseatic League, becoming a major trading hub of the Baltic region.
The Livonian Order completes its conquest of the Semigallians, the last independent Baltic tribe in the region.
The Livonian Confederation collapses during the Livonian War, and Courland becomes a duchy under Polish-Lithuanian suzerainty.
Sweden captures Riga during war with Poland, bringing much of Latvia under Swedish rule.
The Treaty of Altmark confirms Swedish control of Livonia, beginning a period of reforms and relative stability.
Russia captures Riga during the Great Northern War, ending Swedish dominance in the region.
The Treaty of Nystad formally cedes Livonia, including Riga, from Sweden to the Russian Empire.
The Duchy of Courland is annexed by Russia in the Third Partition of Poland, placing all of Latvia under Russian rule.
Serfdom is abolished in Courland, with Livonia following in 1819, freeing Latvian peasants without land.
The first Latvian Song Festival is held in Riga, becoming a defining symbol of national cultural awakening.
The Revolution of 1905 sparks mass uprisings against German landlords and Russian rule across Latvia.
World War I brings German occupation of Courland and the formation of Latvian Riflemen units in the Russian army.
Latvia declares its independence in Riga on November 18 amid the collapse of the Russian and German empires.
The Treaty of Riga ends the War of Independence, with Soviet Russia recognizing Latvian sovereignty.
Latvia adopts its democratic constitution, the Satversme, and is admitted to the League of Nations.
Kārlis Ulmanis stages a coup, dissolving parliament and establishing an authoritarian regime.
The Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact secretly assigns Latvia to the Soviet sphere of influence.
The Soviet Union occupies and annexes Latvia, ending its first period of independence.
Nazi Germany invades and occupies Latvia, leading to the Holocaust that destroys most of the Jewish population.
Soviet forces reconquer most of Latvia, reimposing occupation that lasts for decades.
Mass deportations send tens of thousands of Latvians to Siberia during forced Soviet collectivization.
Public protests against Soviet rule begin under Gorbachev's reforms, marking the start of the Awakening.
Two million people form the Baltic Way human chain across Latvia, Lithuania, and Estonia demanding independence.
The Latvian parliament adopts a declaration on restoring independence, beginning a transition period.
Latvia fully restores its independence in August following the failed Soviet coup and gains UN membership in September.
The last Russian troops withdraw from Latvian territory.
Latvia joins the World Trade Organization and elects Vaira Vīķe-Freiberga as its first female president.
Latvia joins both NATO and the European Union, anchoring itself in Western institutions.
The global financial crisis triggers a severe recession, forcing a major international bailout and austerity.
Latvia adopts the euro as its currency, replacing the lats, and Riga serves as European Capital of Culture.
Latvia holds its first presidency of the Council of the European Union.
Latvia celebrates the centenary of its 1918 declaration of independence.
